questions.prompt: What is the default comms channel for a Critical incident, regardless of time of day?

choices.label: Email only. The timeline is in the email.
choices.rationale: A Critical communicated by email only feels neglectful. The lesson is explicit.

choices.label: Ticket update only. The customer checks it when ready.
choices.rationale: Ticket-only is the Low pattern. Worst possible match for Critical.

choices.label: Phone only. Email creates legal exposure.
choices.rationale: The email follow-up is the durable record. Skipping it leaves no written trail.

questions.explanation: Critical equals phone plus email regardless of time of day.

questions.prompt: Why is calling the customer for every Low "to be safe" a mistake?
choices: Wastes the customer's time on Lows, trains them to dread MSP calls, and lowers the urgency of genuine Critical calls.

choices.label: Huntress charges per outbound call.
choices.rationale: No such commercial mechanic exists.

choices.label: Lows are false positives that should not be communicated at all.
choices.rationale: Lows are real incidents. The issue is the channel, not whether to communicate.

choices.label: Calls about Lows breach customer privacy.
choices.rationale: No such constraint.

questions.explanation: Over-calling devalues the phone channel for the moments it matters.

questions.prompt: The customer's documented preference and the default matrix point to different channels. Which wins?

choices.label: The default matrix wins. Preferences are advisory.
choices.rationale: Inverts the lesson. Customer preferences override the default.

choices: The customer's documented preference overrides the default matrix.

choices.label: The senior decides every channel choice case-by-case.
choices.rationale: The tech makes the routine channel call from the preference. Senior is involved only when the preference does not fit the situation.

choices.label: The most senior person on the call decides.
choices.rationale: The preference is documented, not decided per-call.

questions.explanation: Read the documented preference before deciding the channel. Preferences override the default matrix.

questions.prompt: A Critical lands at a customer whose documented preference is "email only." What do you do?

choices.label: Send only an email. The preference is explicit.
choices.rationale: A Critical-with-email-only-preference is the example of when preference and situation conflict. Surface it.

choices: Bump to senior. The documented preference does not fit the situation; senior decides whether to deviate.

choices.label: Call the customer anyway and apologise for breaching their preference.
choices.rationale: Unilateral deviation without senior sign-off.

choices.label: Wait until business hours and ask the customer their preference again.
choices.rationale: Critical comms does not wait.

questions.explanation: When the preference seems wrong for the situation, surface to senior. The deviation is not a tech-level call.

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson argue that the channel matters more than the message text?
choices: The same message lands differently by channel. Phone implies urgency and engagement; email is digested at the reader's pace; ticket-update only is noticed on next check. Mismatching channel to expected urgency wastes the response work.

choices.label: Customers do not read the message body, only the subject line.
choices.rationale: Inverts the work that goes into well-framed notifications.

choices.label: Message text is automated by the Huntress portal.
choices.rationale: Not factually correct.

choices.label: Phone calls are always recorded; email and tickets are not.
choices.rationale: Recording is not the lesson's framing.

questions.explanation: Channel sets the customer's experience of urgency. Mismatching it wastes good response work.

questions.prompt: When is using all three channels (phone + email + ticket update) the right call?

choices.label: Standard for every High.
choices.rationale: High is phone-or-email plus ticket-update, not all three.

choices.label: Whenever the tech has time to do all three.
choices.rationale: Time-availability is not the criterion.

choices: Reserved for genuinely customer-relationship-significant Criticals where the volume of update is high and the audience is wide.

choices.label: For every customer in their first week to set expectations.
choices.rationale: Not in the lesson.

questions.explanation: All-three-channels is rare and reserved for wide-audience Critical situations.

questions.prompt: Why is phone-plus-email the standard for Critical, rather than phone alone or email alone?

choices.label: Two channels are required by Huntress for compliance.
choices.rationale: No such compliance rule.

choices: Phone confirms awareness and engagement. Email is the durable record the customer forwards or refers back to. Skipping either leaves a gap.

choices.label: Phone is unreliable, so email is the real notification.
choices.rationale: Both have specific roles. Neither is a backup for the other.

choices.label: Email is the urgent channel; phone is the follow-up.
choices.rationale: Reverses the roles entirely.

questions.explanation: Phone for immediate awareness; email for the durable written trail.

questions.prompt: 14:30 Tuesday. High EDR incident contained and resolved. Customer preference: "Highs: phone or email, account manager will route; ticket update on every incident." Which channel for the IT manager?

choices.label: Phone, then email, then ticket update. Three channels for a High.
choices.rationale: Three channels is reserved for Criticals with wide audiences. Over-communicates for a High.

choices: Update the ticket with the full incident note, then send a short email summary. Email is appropriate for a contained High mid-business-day, and the ticket update fulfils the "every incident" requirement.

choices.label: Ticket update only.
choices.rationale: Customer preference includes "phone or email" for Highs in addition to ticket. Ticket-only is the Low pattern.

questions.explanation: Preference says phone or email for Highs (email fits a contained mid-day High) plus ticket on every incident.

questions.prompt: Put these severity levels in order from most channels required (by default) to fewest.
items: High (phone or email per preference + ticket update)
Low (ticket update, optional email, no phone)
questions.explanation: Critical requires the most channels (phone plus email). High is preference-driven with a ticket backup. Low is ticket-only with optional email.

questions.prompt: The IT manager replies by email asking "what does isolation mean, and will it happen often?" What is the best response channel?
choices: Reply by email with a plain-language explanation. An email reply gives the IT manager something to refer back to.

choices.label: Call them to explain in person. Voice is warmer for educational topics.
choices.rationale: Defensible but a step heavier than needed. Switching to phone for an educational follow-up question is over-channelling.

choices.label: Reply with a one-liner: "Isolation just means we contained the host briefly; it's standard."
choices.rationale: Terse responses to genuine customer questions undercut the trust the response built.

questions.explanation: Educational question by email; email reply with the full explanation. The customer can refer back to it.
